groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Subject, type, goal, audience, client, challenge, solution, results, destination, language, length, sources.

groups.title: Audience & Positioning
groups.description: Industry context, profile, decision stage, angle, differentiator, objections, alternatives.

groups.title: Facts & Evidence
groups.description: Baseline, implementation, timeline, metrics, qualitative outcomes, sources, quotes, limitations.

groups.title: Brand & Presentation
groups.description: Tone, voice, perspective, editorial format, terminology, visuals, extra context.

groups.title: Publishing & Conversion
groups.description: CTA, link, SEO keyword, related offer, and optional extras.

groups.title: Privacy, Approval & Compliance
groups.description: Privacy mode, anonymization, approval, regulated topics, jurisdiction, restrictions, as-of date.

variables.label: Case study subject
variables.placeholder: Example: CRM migration for a regional logistics company
variables.hint: Name the product, service, project, campaign, initiative, or change being documented.

help.why: It establishes the central subject and prevents the case study from becoming vague.
help.example: Example: CRM migration for a regional logistics company
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Case study type
variables.placeholder: Select the closest format
variables.hint: Choose the type that best matches the supplied story.

help.why: Different case study types require different framing, evidence language, and conclusions.
help.example: Customer success
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Primary goal
variables.placeholder: Choose the main purpose
variables.hint: Select the most important outcome this case study should support.

help.why: The goal determines what the case study emphasizes and how it concludes.
help.example: Build trust
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Target audience
variables.placeholder: Example: Operations leaders at mid-sized logistics companies evaluating CRM systems
variables.hint: Describe who should read the case study and what they are likely evaluating.

help.why: Audience context materially changes terminology, evidence depth, objections, and calls to action.
help.example: Example: Operations leaders at mid-sized logistics companies evaluating CRM systems
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Client or subject name
variables.placeholder: Example: Northbridge Logistics or Anonymous healthcare provider
variables.hint: Enter the approved public name or a temporary anonymous label.

help.why: The draft requires a consistent way to identify the organization, team, person, or project.
help.example: Example: Northbridge Logistics or Anonymous healthcare provider
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Challenge
variables.placeholder: Describe the situation, problem, constraints, and why action was needed.
variables.hint: Include only challenges that can be stated accurately and publicly.

help.why: A case study needs a clear starting problem against which the solution and results can be understood.
help.example: Describe the situation, problem, constraints, and why action was needed.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Solution provided
variables.placeholder: Explain what was introduced, changed, delivered, or implemented.
variables.hint: Focus on the actual approach rather than broad promotional claims.

help.why: It defines what action produced or contributed to the reported outcome.
help.example: Explain what was introduced, changed, delivered, or implemented.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Results summary
variables.placeholder: List measurable or qualitative outcomes, including timeframes where known.
variables.hint: Use supplied results only; qualitative improvements are acceptable when numbers are unavailable.

help.why: Results are the core evidence of a case study and determine whether claims can be made credibly.
help.example: List measurable or qualitative outcomes, including timeframes where known.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Publication destination
variables.placeholder: Choose the main destination
variables.hint: The structure and formatting will be adapted to the selected destination.

help.why: A website article, sales asset, portfolio story, and internal document require different presentation choices.
help.example: Website page
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Output language
variables.placeholder: Example: English, Ukrainian, German
variables.hint: Specify the language for the complete generated package.

help.why: The output language cannot always be inferred safely from source material.
help.example: English
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Case study length
variables.placeholder: Select the preferred depth
variables.hint: The range applies mainly to the full case study, not editorial notes or optional extras.

help.why: The appropriate level of implementation detail depends on the intended use.
help.example: Standard: 900–1,500 words
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Source material
variables.placeholder: Paste notes, interview excerpts, existing copy, project summaries, or approved source facts.
variables.hint: Include the strongest available factual material. Do not include confidential data without authorization.

help.why: Existing source material improves specificity while reducing the risk of unsupported assumptions.
help.example: Paste notes, interview excerpts, existing copy, project summaries, or approved source facts.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Industry context
variables.placeholder: Describe relevant market conditions, regulations, workflows, or industry pressures.
variables.hint: Add only context that is necessary for readers to understand the challenge.

help.why: Industry context can explain why the challenge mattered without forcing the model to invent market conditions.
help.example: Describe relevant market conditions, regulations, workflows, or industry pressures.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Client or subject profile
variables.placeholder: Example: Team size, business model, location, customer type, or operating environment
variables.hint: Include only profile details approved for use.

help.why: Relevant scale and operating context help readers judge whether the example resembles their situation.
help.example: Example: Team size, business model, location, customer type, or operating environment
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Reader decision stage
variables.placeholder: Choose or let the model decide
variables.hint: Controls how much education, proof, implementation detail, and conversion language to include.

help.why: Readers at different stages need different levels of evidence and product detail.
help.example: Auto
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Story angle
variables.placeholder: Choose the main narrative emphasis
variables.hint: Select an angle only when one part of the story should clearly lead.

help.why: The angle determines narrative order without requiring multiple separate strategy fields.
help.example: Auto
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Key differentiator
variables.placeholder: What was distinctive about the approach, process, expertise, or solution?
variables.hint: Use factual distinctions rather than unsupported claims of superiority.

help.why: It helps the case study communicate why the selected approach was relevant without becoming generic.
help.example: What was distinctive about the approach, process, expertise, or solution?
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Audience objections
variables.placeholder:
Credibility
Cost
Implementation effort
Time to value
Technical fit
Risk or disruption
Team adoption
Proof of results
variables.hint: Choose only concerns that are genuinely relevant to the intended reader. Select all that apply (one per line). Options: Credibility; Cost; Implementation effort; Time to value; Technical fit; Risk or disruption; Team adoption; Proof of results.

help.why: Relevant objections influence which evidence and implementation details should be emphasized.
help.example: Select objections the story should address
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Alternative approaches
variables.placeholder: Mention prior methods, internal processes, or alternatives that may be discussed.
variables.hint: Do not make negative competitor claims unless they are documented and approved.

help.why: Alternatives can clarify the decision without creating unsupported comparisons.
help.example: Mention prior methods, internal processes, or alternatives that may be discussed.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Starting point and baseline
variables.placeholder: Describe the situation before implementation, including baseline metrics when available.
variables.hint: Include dates, measurement periods, definitions, and scope where known.

help.why: Results are more meaningful when readers can understand the initial condition and measurement basis.
help.example: Describe the situation before implementation, including baseline metrics when available.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Implementation details
variables.placeholder: Describe major phases, decisions, integrations, activities, or process changes.
variables.hint: Include enough detail to explain how the solution was applied.

help.why: It distinguishes a credible case study from a simple testimonial or promotional claim.
help.example: Describe major phases, decisions, integrations, activities, or process changes.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Timeline
variables.placeholder: Example: Discovery in January, rollout over eight weeks, results measured after six months
variables.hint: Include only confirmed dates, durations, and milestones.

help.why: Timeframes help readers interpret implementation effort and reported outcomes accurately.
help.example: Example: Discovery in January, rollout over eight weeks, results measured after six months
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Measurable results
variables.placeholder: List metrics with values, periods, units, baselines, and measurement methods when known.
variables.hint: Do not estimate missing percentages or convert qualitative statements into numbers.

help.why: Structured metric input supports accurate results sections and prevents invented performance figures.
help.example: List metrics with values, periods, units, baselines, and measurement methods when known.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Qualitative outcomes
variables.placeholder: Example: Easier onboarding, fewer handoff problems, stronger team confidence
variables.hint: Describe observable non-numeric changes without presenting them as measured statistics.

help.why: Many valid outcomes are important but cannot be represented by a reliable metric.
help.example: Example: Easier onboarding, fewer handoff problems, stronger team confidence
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Evidence and sources
variables.placeholder: List reports, analytics, interviews, documents, surveys, or approved source links.
variables.hint: Identify what supports each important result or factual claim.

help.why: Case-study claims require a clear evidence trail even when public citations are not displayed.
help.example: List reports, analytics, interviews, documents, surveys, or approved source links.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Approved quotations
variables.placeholder: Paste approved quotes with speaker name, role, and organization where permitted.
variables.hint: Only supplied quotations may be used as direct quotes.

help.why: Direct quotations must never be generated or attributed without approved source wording.
help.example: Paste approved quotes with speaker name, role, and organization where permitted.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Limitations and caveats
variables.placeholder: Note data limits, external factors, incomplete measurements, or results that may not generalize.
variables.hint: Include anything readers need to interpret the outcome fairly.

help.why: Transparent limitations improve credibility and reduce misleading causal claims.
help.example: Note data limits, external factors, incomplete measurements, or results that may not generalize.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Tone
variables.placeholder: Select the preferred tone
variables.hint: The tone affects delivery but never overrides accuracy or evidence requirements.

help.why: Tone changes how the same evidence is presented to different audiences.
help.example: Clear and credible
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Brand voice
variables.placeholder: Describe preferred voice, sentence style, formality, and examples of wording to follow.
variables.hint: Leave blank for a natural, professional, evidence-led voice.

help.why: Specific brand guidance improves consistency without forcing several separate style fields.
help.example: Describe preferred voice, sentence style, formality, and examples of wording to follow.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Narrative perspective
variables.placeholder: Choose how the story should be told
variables.hint: Interview-led output still uses only supplied and approved quotations.

help.why: Perspective materially affects voice, attribution, and the relationship between narrator and subject.
help.example: Third-person
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Editorial format
variables.placeholder: Select the presentation style
variables.hint: Choose the structure that best suits the destination and reader.

help.why: A technical implementation report and a human-centered transformation story should not share an identical structure.
help.example: Balanced narrative
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Terminology preferences
variables.placeholder: List preferred terms, capitalization, product names, and wording to avoid.
variables.hint: Use this for brand terminology rather than general writing instructions.

help.why: Accurate names and approved terminology are important in client-facing business content.
help.example: List preferred terms, capitalization, product names, and wording to avoid.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Available visual evidence
variables.placeholder: Example: Dashboard screenshots, process photos, charts, diagrams, before-and-after interface views
variables.hint: List real assets that may support visual callout recommendations.

help.why: Visual recommendations should be based on assets that actually exist.
help.example: Example: Dashboard screenshots, process photos, charts, diagrams, before-and-after interface views
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Additional context
variables.placeholder: Add any relevant requirement not covered elsewhere.
variables.hint: Use one place for important miscellaneous context, exclusions, or preferences.

help.why: It captures meaningful exceptions without creating several low-value specialist fields.
help.example: Add any relevant requirement not covered elsewhere.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Call-to-action goal
variables.placeholder: Choose the preferred next step
variables.hint: Use one proportional action that fits the case study and reader stage.

help.why: The closing action should reflect the actual conversion goal rather than a generic sales request.
help.example: Auto
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: CTA destination
variables.placeholder: https://example.com/next-step
variables.hint: Enter the real destination. The model must not invent a URL.

help.why: A usable CTA requires an accurate destination when one is available.
help.example: https://example.com/next-step
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Preferred CTA wording
variables.placeholder: Example: See how the workflow could fit your team
variables.hint: Leave blank for an automatically written, non-aggressive CTA.

help.why: Some brands require approved action wording or a specific conversion phrase.
help.example: Example: See how the workflow could fit your team
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Primary SEO keyword
variables.placeholder: Example: logistics CRM implementation case study
variables.hint: Use only when the case study will be published on a discoverable web page.

help.why: A supplied search phrase can guide metadata and headings without turning the case study into keyword-stuffed copy.
help.example: Example: logistics CRM implementation case study
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Related offer
variables.placeholder: Describe the service, product, consultation, resource, or next step connected to the CTA.
variables.hint: Include only real offer details and avoid unsupported promises.

help.why: The CTA should connect naturally to an actual offer rather than introduce an invented service.
help.example: Describe the service, product, consultation, resource, or next step connected to the CTA.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Optional output extras
variables.placeholder:
SEO metadata
Visual callouts
Social teaser
One-page summary
Sales slide summary
Pull quotes
variables.hint: Choose only assets that will be used with the case study. Select all that apply (one per line). Options: SEO metadata; Visual callouts; Social teaser; One-page summary; Sales slide summary; Pull quotes.

help.why: It allows useful repurposing without adding every possible asset to every output.
help.example: Select reusable assets to include
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Privacy mode
variables.placeholder: Choose how identities should appear
variables.hint: Select anonymization when public use of names or identifying details is not approved.

help.why: Case studies frequently contain client, employee, project, or commercially sensitive identities.
help.example: Use supplied names
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Anonymization instructions
variables.placeholder: Specify replacement labels and details that must be generalized, removed, or preserved.
variables.hint: Do not include clues that would unintentionally identify the subject.

help.why: Different anonymization workflows require explicit control over what remains identifiable.
help.example: Specify replacement labels and details that must be generalized, removed, or preserved.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Approval status
variables.placeholder: Select the current approval level
variables.hint: The output will distinguish draft material from content approved for publication.

help.why: The model must not imply that facts, quotes, or publication rights have been approved when they have not.
help.example: Draft—approval pending
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Regulated or sensitive topic
variables.placeholder: Select only when relevant
variables.hint: Used to apply cautious language and identify claims requiring authoritative review.

help.why: High-stakes case studies require stronger qualification and jurisdiction-aware review.
help.example: None
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Relevant jurisdiction
variables.placeholder: Example: United Kingdom, California, European Union
variables.hint: Specify the country, state, region, or regulatory environment that may affect claims.

help.why: Legal, financial, medical, employment, and similar requirements can differ by jurisdiction.
help.example: Example: United Kingdom, California, European Union
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Restrictions and disclosures
variables.placeholder: List prohibited claims, required disclosures, relationship disclosures, or confidentiality limits.
variables.hint: Include only applicable restrictions rather than generic legal language.

help.why: The case study may require specific claim exclusions, commercial disclosures, or confidentiality protections.
help.example: List prohibited claims, required disclosures, relationship disclosures, or confidentiality limits.
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.

variables.label: Information current as of
variables.placeholder: YYYY-MM-DD
variables.hint: Useful when results, product capabilities, team details, or project status may change.

help.why: A factual cutoff prevents changing results or capabilities from being presented as permanently current.
help.example: YYYY-MM-DD
help.avoid: Invented metrics, fabricated quotes, unsupported superiority claims, or private details without approval.
